title.en: Health or Life Insurance Claim Rejected? Ombudsman Route and the Grounds That Fail

answerBox.en: Health and life insurance rejections follow a fixed escalation: written complaint to the insurer's grievance cell (15 days), IRDAI's Bima Bharosa portal, then the Insurance Ombudsman — free, within 1 year, for claims up to ₹50 lakh. Key protections: after 8 years of premiums (5 for health per current norms) policies become largely incontestable, and "pre-existing disease" rejections fail without proof of concealment.

en.title: Attack the stated ground, not the rejection
en.summary: PED, non-disclosure, waiting period, "not medically necessary".
en.detail: Common grounds and their weaknesses: **pre-existing disease (PED)** — fails unless the insurer proves you *knew and concealed* it at proposal; **non-disclosure** — must be material and within the contestability window; **waiting periods** — check the policy's actual schedule against your dates; **"treatment not medically necessary"** — countered by the treating doctor's certificate. Get the **complete rejection letter and policy wording**; vague repudiations are themselves a deficiency.

en.title: Know the moratorium shields
en.detail: Two heavyweight protections: **Section 45, Insurance Act** — a life policy cannot be questioned after **3 years** on any ground except proven fraud; and health insurance's **moratorium** — after **5 years of continuous coverage** (current IRDAI norms, previously 8), no claim can be contested for non-disclosure/PED except proven fraud. If your policy has crossed these lines, say so in the first written complaint — many repudiations die on this alone.

en.title: Escalate: grievance cell → Bima Bharosa → Ombudsman
en.summary: The same free ladder as all insurance disputes.
en.detail: Written complaint to the insurer's **Grievance Redressal Officer** (15-day clock), then **[Bima Bharosa](https://bimabharosa.irdai.gov.in)** (IRDAI tracking), then the **[Insurance Ombudsman](https://www.cioins.co.in)** within 1 year — free, no lawyer, awards up to **₹50 lakh** binding on the insurer. Attach the treating hospital's records and doctor certificates; medical-opinion disputes are decided on documents, and the insurer's panel-doctor opinion is not privileged over your treating doctor's.

en.title: Consumer commission for the big/harassment cases
en.summary: Claims above ₹50L, death claims, bad-faith conduct.
en.detail: For larger claims, death-claim disputes, or where you seek harassment compensation beyond the policy amount, the **consumer commission** route (our [consumer forum guide](/help/consumer-complaints/consumer-forum-complaint-guide)) is the forum — deficiency in service squarely covers wrongful repudiation, and commissions award claim + interest + compensation + costs. A ₹999 **advocate's notice** first; nominees file death claims with the same escalation rights.

en.footnote: Never accept a partial "ex-gratia" settlement without advice — accepting in full-and-final can extinguish the balance claim. Cashless denial at the hospital is not claim rejection; pay, preserve, and claim reimbursement with the same rights.

en: health insurance claim rejected what to do

en.q: The insurer says my illness was pre-existing. Can they reject on that?
en.a: Only if the policy's PED waiting period genuinely applies AND they can show the condition existed and was known/concealed at proposal. Undiagnosed conditions you were unaware of are not concealment, and after the 5-year moratorium (continuous coverage), PED rejection needs proven fraud. Demand their evidence — often there is none beyond an underwriter's inference.

en.q: What is Section 45 and how does it protect a life insurance nominee?
en.a: A life policy cannot be called in question after 3 years from issuance/revival on grounds of misstatement — only proven fraud survives, with the burden on the insurer. Death-claim repudiations of old policies citing "non-disclosure" routinely fail on Section 45; quote it in the first complaint.

en.q: Cashless was denied at the hospital. Is my claim over?
en.a: No — cashless denial is a pre-authorisation decision, not a claim decision. Pay (or use the hospital's escalation), preserve every record and bill, and file for reimbursement; a later rejection then enters the normal grievance → Ombudsman → commission ladder with full rights.

en.q: How long does the Ombudsman take, and is the award binding?
en.a: Recommendations/awards typically come within 3 months of a complete complaint; the award binds the insurer (payable within 30 days) but not you — if unsatisfied, the consumer commission remains open. It is the best effort-to-outcome ratio in insurance disputes.

en.q: The insurer deducted half the bill as "unreasonable charges". Can they?
en.a: Deductions must trace to policy terms (room-rent capping proportionality, listed exclusions, sub-limits). Arbitrary "reasonable and customary" slashes are challengeable — itemise the deductions against the policy schedule in your complaint; ombudsmen restore unjustified cuts regularly.

reviewer: Adv. Priya Sharma, Bar Council of Delhi, D/1284/2016
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
